commander_summary: RWDN is operationally functional — all four pumps running, pressure healthy at 68.5 PSI, reservoir full at 92.4% — but the thesis is CONTESTED because three of five kill switches cannot be fully evaluated due to data gaps, and the system is accumulating stress pre-conditions. The most critical unknown is historical baseline pressure: if it was ~85 PSI, the system may already be near-triggering Kill Switch #5 (Divergent Inventory Paradox) while surface indicators look healthy. Pump 4 seal degradation will eliminate system redundancy during teardown, leaving only 13.5 percentage points of headroom to Kill Switch #1 going into peak summer demand. Recommendation is INCREASE_MONITORING with top priority on acquiring baseline pressure data, P2/P3 vibration telemetry, and P4 teardown timeline — resolving these three unknowns will either confirm system health or reveal a thesis that is substantially weaker than surface data suggests.
score_reasoning: INCREASE_MONITORING is the only action consistent with CONTESTED thesis status. The system is not in crisis — no kill switch is breached and all measurable indicators are healthy — which rules out REDUCE_LOAD or EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. But the convergence of monitoring gaps, approaching P4 maintenance, and seasonal demand escalation means MAINTAIN_OPERATIONS provides insufficient visibility into developing risks. Priority monitoring actions: (1) obtain historical baseline pressure to resolve Kill Switch #5 status, (2) obtain P2/P3 vibration data, (3) confirm P4 teardown timeline, (4) obtain multi-sector hydrant pressure readings.

chain: Reservoir >90% places system in Kill Switch #5 precondition zone — only unknown baseline pressure determines if kill switch is near-triggered

compounding_risks.outcome: The system's strongest surface evidence (full reservoir at 92.4%, healthy pressure at 68.5 PSI, all pumps running) simultaneously creates its greatest vulnerability — the reservoir level places the system in Kill Switch #5's precondition zone, the healthy pressure prevents oversight intervention, and all-pumps-running masks the fact that one unit is actively degrading and will remove redundancy when taken offline. The system looks healthiest at precisely the moment it is accumulating pre-conditions for its most dangerous failure mode.

Per LZ-EH-003, MWA's actions (maintenance log documenting degradation) carry more evidentiary weight than their public statement. Per LZ-EH-005, this is a genuine contradiction: 'operating optimally' vs 'seal weepage requiring teardown.' However, per null hypothesis analysis and LZ-RC-004, the contradiction is best explained by standard institutional communication norms rather than strategic deception. The paradox is held because BOTH are simultaneously true: the system IS currently functional (data is correct) AND the system has an active degradation issue that is not being communicated externally (behavior is informative). Resolving in either direction would lose valid information.

Per LZ-EPH-001, 'I don't know' is the correct output for Kill Switch #5 status. The current pressure is verifiably healthy relative to Kill Switch #4 (absolute threshold), but its status relative to Kill Switch #5 (relative threshold) is genuinely unknown. Forcing resolution in either direction would require assuming a baseline, which violates LZ-EPH-002 (confidence must be proportional to evidence). The paradox is: 68.5 PSI is simultaneously reassuring (well above 40 PSI) and potentially alarming (possibly 19%+ below historical baseline). This cannot be resolved without data.

bias_check.assessment: The thesis is CONTESTED because evidence genuinely pulls in both directions simultaneously. Surface operational data supports the thesis: all pumps running, healthy pressure, full reservoir, clean water quality. But the monitoring infrastructure reveals three of five kill switches cannot be fully evaluated, one unit is actively degrading toward a maintenance event that eliminates redundancy margin, and the oversight framework operates on incomplete information. Neither STABLE (which would ignore the monitoring gaps and P4 stress chain) nor WEAKENING (which would overweight prospective risks against currently healthy measurements) accurately captures this state. The paradox is real and must be held, not forced.

kill_switches.name: 1. Redundant Load Threshold (>115% per active pump for >48hr)
kill_switches.detail: Currently GREEN on measurable data but MONITORING because the 3-pump scenario is approaching and the margin math is tight. Escalation warranted: need P4 teardown timeline and summer demand projections to assess whether this kill switch will be approached during the maintenance window.

kill_switches.name: 2. Cavitation Threshold (>35 Hz vibration at >95% load)
kill_switches.detail: WARNING rather than MONITORING because the inability to evaluate 50% of the fleet against this kill switch is a genuine monitoring infrastructure failure. The two units we CAN measure are safely below threshold, but we cannot make any determination for P2/P3. Per LZ-RC-003, their health cannot be assumed from the absence of data.

kill_switches.name: 3. Fire Flow Collapse (<20 PSI sector hydrant)
kill_switches.detail: WARNING because the thesis makes a system-wide claim ('all municipal sectors') that can only be verified for one sector. Sector B is healthy at 65 PSI (225% above kill switch threshold), but other sectors are unverifiable. The warning is about monitoring coverage, not about any observed degradation.

kill_switches.name: 4. Regulatory Pressure Breach (<40 PSI system)
kill_switches.detail: 68.5 PSI provides substantial margin. Even in a 3-pump scenario, system pressure is maintained by pump output, not pump count alone — pressure loss would require significant demand increase or main break beyond current evidence. GREEN is appropriate.

kill_switches.name: 5. Divergent Inventory Paradox (Reservoir >90% AND Pressure >20% below baseline)
kill_switches.detail: WARNING because one half of the trigger condition is already met (reservoir >90%) and the other half is completely unverifiable. Per LZ-EPH-001, the status of this kill switch is genuinely unknown. The illustrative math showing a plausible near-breach scenario (85 PSI baseline) prevents GREEN classification. This is the top priority data acquisition target. Per LZ-EH-004, the hypothetical 85 PSI baseline should be treated as a scenario to investigate, not as evidence of a near-breach.

unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Historical baseline system pressure data from MWA SCADA records or SPUC regulatory filings. A baseline of ~80+ PSI would be alarming. A baseline of ~65-72 PSI would be reassuring.

unresolved_tensions.description: P4 teardown timeline is unconfirmed. The window between P4 going offline and P4 returning to service is the period of maximum thesis vulnerability. Duration and timing relative to peak demand are unknown.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Firm scheduled date for P4 teardown in MWA maintenance records, expected outage duration, and whether the timeline precedes or overlaps with sustained temperatures above 85°F.

unresolved_tensions.description: P2/P3 mechanical health is unverifiable. If either unit has developing vibration issues that coincide with P4 downtime, the redundancy math deteriorates catastrophically (2-pump operation at ~152% per unit, immediately breaching Kill Switch #1).
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: P2/P3 vibration telemetry. Any reading above 20 Hz on either unit would significantly escalate the compound stress assessment.

unresolved_tensions.description: Fire Dispatch independence is unverified. If Fire Dispatch relies on MWA for system status information, their silence provides zero independent verification — the entire oversight chain has a single point of information control at MWA.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Evidence of independent pressure monitoring capability at Fire Dispatch, or absence thereof.

monitoring_triggers: CRITICAL: Historical baseline system pressure obtained — if >80 PSI, immediately re-evaluate Kill Switch #5 and escalate to potential thesis falsification review
HIGH: P2 or P3 vibration data obtained — if >20 Hz on either unit, escalate compound stress to CRITICAL and reassess redundancy math
HIGH: P4 teardown date confirmed — if scheduled during peak demand season (sustained >85°F), escalate Kill Switch #1 proximity assessment
HIGH: P4 teardown date NOT confirmed within 2 weeks — treat as evidence of deferral pattern and increase bear pressure by 5-8 points
MEDIUM: Multi-sector hydrant pressure data obtained — if any sector below 40 PSI, escalate Kill Switch #3 to WARNING/TRIGGERED
MEDIUM: Ambient temperature sustained above 85°F — triggers seasonal demand loop activation, reassess all pump load projections
LOW: Fire Dispatch tanker request or mutual aid activation — treat as high-fidelity independent signal of distribution failure, immediate thesis re-evaluation
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: Historical baseline system pressure. This single data point determines whether Kill Switch #5 is near-triggered or safely distant, and could swing the entire assessment from 'healthy system with monitoring gaps' to 'system potentially in early-stage cascading failure.' No other unknown has comparable asymmetric impact.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Obtaining historical baseline pressure of >80 PSI would move the assessment toward WEAKENING/potential falsification (Kill Switch #5 near-breach). Obtaining baseline of ~65-72 PSI plus healthy P2/P3 vibration data plus multi-sector healthy hydrant readings would move assessment to STABLE with monitoring gaps closed. Confirmation of P4 teardown delay into peak summer would move compound stress to CRITICAL and bear pressure to 45+.
_layer4_raw.final_report: RWDN is operationally functional — all four pumps running, pressure healthy at 68.5 PSI, reservoir full at 92.4% — but the thesis is CONTESTED because three of five kill switches cannot be fully evaluated due to data gaps, and the system is accumulating stress pre-conditions. The most critical unknown is historical baseline pressure: if it was ~85 PSI, the system may already be near-triggering Kill Switch #5 (Divergent Inventory Paradox) while surface indicators look healthy. Pump 4 seal degradation will eliminate system redundancy during teardown, leaving only 13.5 percentage points of headroom to Kill Switch #1 going into peak summer demand. Recommendation is INCREASE_MONITORING with top priority on acquiring baseline pressure data, P2/P3 vibration telemetry, and P4 teardown timeline — resolving these three unknowns will either confirm system health or reveal a thesis that is substantially weaker than surface data suggests.

player_analysis.stated_position: System operating optimally. Regional reservoir levels remain strong going into the summer.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Internal maintenance log documents Pump 4 seal weepage and schedules routine teardown. P4 is running at the highest load (78.5%) and highest vibration (18.5 Hz) in the fleet. No public disclosure of P4 degradation. No evidence of expedited maintenance scheduling or emergency procurement of seal components.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: Clear and verifiable. Per LZ-EH-003, the internal maintenance log (action/documentation) directly contradicts 'operating optimally.' A system with an active seal failure requiring teardown on a load-bearing unit is not optimal by any engineering definition. Additionally, MWA is NOT doing several things a proactively managed utility would: (1) not communicating P4 status to stakeholders, (2) not providing a teardown timeline, (3) not showing evidence of accelerated repair scheduling despite approaching peak demand season. The absence of urgency in their actions is itself a behavioral signal.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: MWA is managing narrative first, operations second. Their incentive structure (continuity of service, public reassurance) explains the say-do gap without needing to invoke malice — they are optimizing for public confidence and regulatory non-intervention. Disclosing P4 issues would invite SPUC scrutiny, public concern, and potentially mandatory action on a timeline MWA doesn't control. By characterizing the system as 'optimal' and the teardown as 'routine,' they preserve operational discretion over timing. This is consistent with the thesis's characterization of MWA as 'historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs' — though that characterization itself is unverified for the current case (per Layer 2 SIG-014).

player_analysis.stated_position: No public position detected in current data cycle.
player_analysis.observed_actions: No regulatory engagement, no audits, no inquiries visible. Per thesis, SPUC monitors macro indicators (reservoir, pressure) but lacks real-time telemetry.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: Not a say-do gap per se — SPUC is doing exactly what its structural limitations predict. The macro indicators it monitors (reservoir 92.4%, pressure 68.5 PSI) both read healthy. SPUC would have no reason to act based on what it can see. The gap is between what SPUC SHOULD be monitoring (pump-level health, vibration, seal integrity) and what it CAN monitor. This is a structural capability gap, not a behavioral one.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: SPUC is operating within its design parameters — which are inadequate for detecting the current threat. There is no evidence SPUC is being strategically passive; it is simply instrumented for the wrong failure mode. It monitors for reservoir depletion and gross pressure loss, not for mechanical degradation of distribution assets. Given MWA's information management (SIG-008), SPUC likely does not know about P4 seal weepage. Without independent telemetry, SPUC functions as a lagging indicator at best.

player_analysis.observed_actions: No tanker requests, no mutual aid activations, no observable concern signals.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: No gap detectable — but the evidentiary value of their silence is severely limited. Per LZ-RC-003, absence of dispatch activity is not evidence of system health. The critical unknown is whether Fire Dispatch has independent pressure monitoring or relies on MWA reporting. If the latter, their silence simply mirrors MWA's curated narrative and provides zero independent verification.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Most likely operating on incomplete information. Fire Dispatch is an end-user, not an operator — they react to observed pressure failures at the hydrant level, not to upstream mechanical degradation. P4 seal weepage would not manifest as a hydrant-level pressure drop until the failure progresses significantly (P4 is still online). Fire Dispatch's silence is therefore temporally expected — they would only signal AFTER a failure propagates to distribution, not before.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Degradation → Forced Outage → Load Redistribution → Accelerated Degradation of Remaining Units
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: P4 forced offline (either by planned teardown or unplanned seal failure). Three remaining pumps absorb ~101.5% load each. If any additional unit shows degradation under sustained higher load, the loop self-reinforces: higher load → faster wear → next unit failure → even higher load on survivors. The loop transitions from dormant to active_orderly when P4 goes offline, and from active_orderly to active_accelerating if sustained 3-pump operation persists into peak summer demand.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the primary mechanical threat to the thesis. The thesis asserts 'sufficient mechanical redundancy' — but redundancy is only meaningful if the spare capacity can be deployed without pushing remaining units into stress ranges that accelerate their own failure. With P4 offline, the system has zero redundancy margin and operates in a zone where any additional failure cascades toward Kill Switch #1.

feedback_loops.loop: Information Asymmetry → Delayed Oversight Response → Extended Degradation Window
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop is ALREADY ACTIVE. MWA is framing the system as optimal; SPUC lacks telemetry to independently verify; Fire Dispatch likely depends on MWA or observable hydrant pressure (which currently reads fine). The consequence: the external oversight ecosystem is operating on stale/curated information. This extends the window during which P4 degradation can progress without triggering external intervention. The loop transitions to active_accelerating if MWA delays the teardown into summer peak and no external party detects or challenges the delay.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The thesis implicitly relies on a functioning oversight stack (MWA monitors → SPUC regulates → Fire Dispatch provides independent signal). Layer 2 evidence shows this stack has a single point of information control: MWA. If MWA filters upstream, the entire oversight chain degrades. This doesn't falsify the thesis directly, but it means the thesis's safety nets are less reliable than assumed.

feedback_loops.loop: Seasonal Demand Escalation → Load Increase → Reduced Headroom → Kill Switch #1 Proximity
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Currently at 72°F with loads averaging 76%. As temperatures rise and summer demand increases, baseline loads will rise even with all four pumps operational. The loop activates when background demand pushes per-unit loads above 85% (the upper boundary of normal range). It compounds with the first feedback loop if P4 is simultaneously offline. Specific trigger: sustained ambient temperatures above ~85-90°F (typical summer peak) would be the observable catalyst, though the exact load-temperature relationship is unquantified in available data.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is a temporal compounding risk. Neither the current load levels nor the seasonal trend alone threaten the thesis. But the convergence of rising demand + reduced fleet capacity creates a window where Kill Switch #1 could be approached. The thesis is most vulnerable during the overlap period of P4 downtime and peak demand.

behavioral_patterns.pattern: Asymmetric information flow: operator controls narrative while oversight entities lack independent verification capability
All external-facing indicators are healthy while internal operational data shows degradation — creating a false-calm surface
Regulator structurally blind to the specific failure mode developing (pump-level mechanical degradation), monitors only for gross systemic failure

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: MWA public statement omits Pump 4 maintenance issue (SIG-008)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: MWA's public statement is a routine update not intended to address granular pump-level status. The omission reflects standard communication practice (utilities don't report every maintenance item publicly), not strategic information suppression.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA is engaging in standard institutional communication — general reassurance statements are their default mode and individual pump seal issues would not typically rise to public disclosure level. The word 'optimally' is marketing language, not an engineering assessment.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: MWA is deliberately suppressing P4 information to avoid regulatory scrutiny or public concern during a sensitive pre-summer period, buying time to manage the repair on their own timeline.
evidence_citations: Maintenance log documenting seal weepage (Tier 1 originating source)
Public statement claiming 'operating optimally' (Tier 1 public communication)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If null holds: MWA proceeds with teardown on a normal schedule and discloses P4 status in next regulatory filing. If alternative: MWA delays teardown past start of peak season with no regulatory notification.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: While LZ-EH-003 correctly identifies that the maintenance log carries more evidentiary weight than the public statement, the most parsimonious explanation (LZ-RC-004) is that utilities routinely issue optimistic public statements and this is not strategically motivated. The contradiction between 'optimally' and 'seal weepage' is real and should be flagged, but it is more likely institutional communication norms than deliberate suppression. However: this null hypothesis classification does NOT diminish the practical consequence — regardless of WHY MWA omits P4 data, the effect is that external oversight operates on incomplete information.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Missing vibration telemetry for Pumps 2 and 3 (SIG-003)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The missing data reflects a mundane instrumentation gap — sensors not installed, not calibrated, temporarily offline, or data not included in the feed provided. No strategic significance.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Sensor coverage gaps are common in municipal infrastructure. Not all units may be instrumented to the same level. This is a resourcing/infrastructure limitation, not a strategic choice.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Data exists but was not included in the reporting feed, either through selection bias or because the readings would reveal concerns.
evidence_citations: P1 vibration data present at 12.1 Hz (confirming some sensors exist)
P4 vibration data present at 18.5 Hz (confirming selective coverage)
P2 and P3 vibration data absent
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Request P2/P3 vibration data. If it exists and was simply not included: instrumentation exists and the gap is correctable. If it does not exist: confirms structural monitoring gap requiring sensor installation.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The simplest explanation (LZ-RC-004) is that the data isn't available due to infrastructure limitations. The fact that P1 and P4 HAVE vibration data while P2 and P3 do not could indicate uneven sensor deployment (e.g., P4 was instrumented due to prior concerns, P1 as a reference unit). Regardless of cause, the practical impact is identical: 50% of the fleet is blind to cavitation monitoring.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Pump 4 degradation + maintenance + seasonal timing convergence (SIG-002, SIG-007, SIG-011)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: P4 seal weepage is a normal maintenance item, teardown is scheduled, and MWA will execute the repair on a reasonable timeline. The seasonal timing is coincidental — pumps require maintenance year-round and sometimes that coincides with demand season.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Seal weepage is a known failure mode for centrifugal pumps and 'routine teardown' is the appropriate response. The timing approaching summer is notable but not evidence of strategic behavior — it's just when the seal failed.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: MWA has been aware of P4 degradation for longer than the maintenance log suggests and deferred action, consistent with the thesis's characterization of their delay pattern. The 'routine' framing minimizes urgency.
evidence_citations: Maintenance log: seal weepage documented, teardown scheduled
P4 vibration 18.5 Hz (53% above P1 baseline but below concern thresholds)
P4 load 78.5% (highest in fleet but within normal range)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: P4 teardown commenced and completed before sustained temperatures above 85°F. Alternatively: teardown delayed past onset of peak demand, confirming the delay pattern.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis is that this is simply a pump needing maintenance at a somewhat inconvenient time. The convergence with summer approach is a RISK FACTOR but not evidence of strategic behavior by any player. The thesis implication is real regardless of strategic intent — whether MWA delays deliberately or accidentally, the system is vulnerable during P4 downtime. The null hypothesis holding does not reduce the operational risk; it simply means the risk is mechanical/temporal rather than behavioral.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: No baseline pressure data for Kill Switch #5 evaluation (SIG-013)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Baseline pressure data exists in MWA's historical records but was not included in the current data feed. This is a data availability gap, not evidence of a problem.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The current data feed is a snapshot. Historical baselines would be in MWA's SCADA/operations databases. Their absence from this feed is most likely a reporting scope limitation.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Baseline pressure data is unavailable because MWA does not maintain robust historical records, reflecting broader data management deficiencies.
evidence_citations: System pressure 68.5 PSI (current measurement exists, confirming active pressure monitoring)
Reservoir Alpha 92.4% (precondition for Kill Switch #5 is met)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Request historical system pressure data from MWA records. If baseline is ~68-72 PSI: Kill Switch #5 is clearly not triggered (minimal decline). If baseline is ~80-85+ PSI: Kill Switch #5 may be near-triggered or triggered, fundamentally changing the assessment.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-EPH-001, the correct output regarding Kill Switch #5 status is 'I don't know.' The baseline gap is almost certainly a data feed limitation, not a strategic signal. But the CONSEQUENCE of this gap is severe: the system is in the precondition zone (reservoir >90%) and the status of the key criterion (>20% pressure drop) is completely unknown. The null hypothesis addresses WHY the data is missing (mundane reasons). It does not address the risk that the missing data might reveal.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Only one sector's hydrant pressure reported (SIG-005)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Sector B was the only sector with a recent hydrant test or pressure reading in the data feed. Other sectors may have been tested at different times and data simply isn't in this snapshot.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Hydrant pressure testing is typically conducted on rotating schedules by fire departments or water utilities. A single sector reading in any given data cycle is normal operational practice, not a monitoring failure.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Only Sector B has functioning pressure monitoring, and other sectors are genuinely unmonitored.
evidence_citations: Thesis claims fire flow capability 'across ALL municipal sectors'
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Request hydrant pressure data from all sectors. If available: gap closes and Kill Switch #3 can be system-wide evaluated. If unavailable: confirms structural monitoring gap.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The simplest explanation is that this is a snapshot limitation. But as with SIG-013, the null hypothesis about WHY data is missing doesn't resolve the practical problem: Kill Switch #3 cannot be evaluated system-wide. The thesis makes a system-wide claim that currently has single-sector verification.

hidden_moves.likely_action: MWA is likely procuring seal replacement parts and scheduling contractor/crew availability for the P4 teardown, but has not committed to a firm date — preserving flexibility to defer if budget or staffing conflicts arise.
hidden_moves.incentive_basis: MWA's institutional incentive is to manage the repair on their timeline, minimizing operational disruption and cost. Scheduling a teardown without committing to a date maintains the appearance of responsiveness while preserving deferral optionality. This is standard institutional behavior for non-emergency maintenance items.
hidden_moves.evidence_hints: Maintenance log says 'scheduled for routine teardown' without specifying a date. The word 'routine' frames the issue as non-urgent, which is inconsistent with the combination of seal weepage + highest fleet vibration + approaching peak season.

hidden_moves.likely_action: MWA has NOT proactively notified SPUC or Fire Dispatch of P4 seal weepage, relying on the fact that macro indicators (pressure, reservoir) are healthy and will not trigger regulatory inquiry.
hidden_moves.incentive_basis: Voluntary disclosure of equipment degradation invites scrutiny, potential mandatory timelines, and public concern. With macro indicators reading healthy, MWA has no regulatory obligation to disclose pump-level maintenance items (assuming SPUC's mandate is macro-level as described in thesis). Rational institutional behavior is to handle it internally until it either resolves or escalates to a level that triggers mandatory reporting.
hidden_moves.evidence_hints: MWA public statement omits P4 entirely. SPUC shows no activity. Fire Dispatch shows no activity. If MWA had disclosed, we would expect to see at least some acknowledgment in one of these channels. The absence across all external oversight channels is consistent with non-disclosure.
hidden_moves.materialization_signal: If MWA has disclosed: we would see SPUC inquiry notes, Fire Dispatch contingency planning, or updated public communications referencing P4. If non-disclosure continues: silence persists until P4 is either repaired or fails.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: All current measurable indicators are within healthy ranges: pumps operational at 74-78% load, system pressure at 68.5 PSI (71% margin to kill switch), reservoir at 92.4%, water quality clean. No kill switch is currently breached on available data. If P4 teardown is executed promptly, remaining pumps can handle the load at ~101.5% (below 115% threshold), and system returns to full redundancy post-repair.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Confirmed if: P4 teardown completed before peak summer demand (est. 4-8 weeks), AND remaining pumps show no degradation during the window, AND missing data (P2/P3 vibration, other sector hydrants, baseline pressure) when obtained shows healthy readings.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis's claim of 'robust structural integrity and sufficient mechanical redundancy' likely requires qualification. The system has operational capacity but NOT the monitoring infrastructure to verify its own safety claims. A modified thesis would read: 'RWDN maintains adequate current operational performance, but its monitoring and oversight architecture is insufficient to provide early warning of mechanical degradation, creating windows of undetected vulnerability particularly during maintenance events coinciding with peak demand.' The redundancy claim specifically needs modification — redundancy exists only when all four pumps are healthy, and the system has no headroom for overlapping failures.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: Three of five kill switches unmonitorable due to data gaps (SIG-003, SIG-005, SIG-013). P4 degradation reduces effective redundancy to zero during teardown. Information asymmetry between MWA and oversight entities. System operates in upper-normal load range before peak season.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: No kill switch is currently breached on available data. Falsification would require: (1) baseline pressure data revealing Kill Switch #5 is near-triggered or breached (plausible but unverified — the 85 PSI hypothetical shows this is possible), OR (2) P4 failure cascading into 3-pump operation during peak demand where loads exceed 115% for >48 hours, OR (3) other sector hydrant pressures already below 20 PSI (possible but no evidence for or against). The 15% reflects the genuine uncertainty created by the monitoring gaps — we cannot rule out that a kill switch is already breached behind the data blind spots.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Baseline pressure data showing >85 PSI historically (which would put current 68.5 PSI at >19% decline, near Kill Switch #5 trigger with reservoir already >90%). Or: P2/P3 vibration data showing readings above 25 Hz concern threshold. Or: non-Sector-B hydrant pressures below 40 PSI.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly. All measurable indicators are within normal ranges and no cascading failures are in progress. However, the system is accumulating pre-conditions for disorderly stress: P4 degradation progressing, maintenance window approaching, seasonal demand rising, monitoring gaps preventing early warning. The stress is building beneath a healthy-looking surface. The distinction between orderly and disorderly here is temporal — the system is orderly because nothing has broken yet, not because the developing conditions are being actively managed.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The primary catalyst for transition from orderly to disorderly is P4 forced outage (unplanned failure rather than controlled teardown). If P4 seal failure progresses from weepage to catastrophic during a demand peak, the system loses a unit without controlled preparation — remaining pumps absorb shock load, potentially exceeding 101.5% if demand is elevated. Secondary catalyst: a concurrent failure on P2 or P3 (which we cannot detect due to missing vibration data) during P4's planned maintenance window. The monitoring gaps mean we would not see the secondary catalyst developing. Estimated proximity: the system could operate in current mode for weeks without a catalyst, but the window narrows as temperatures rise and P4 degradation progresses.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: Two legs are pre-loaded: (1) Reservoir >90% places the system in Kill Switch #5's precondition zone — only the pressure criterion (baseline unknown) determines whether this kill switch is near-triggered; (2) P4 degradation is progressing on a timeline that intersects with seasonal demand increase. Neither pre-load is sufficient alone to threaten the thesis, but they create a configuration where a single additional stress (demand spike, second pump issue, or main break) could push multiple kill switches simultaneously. The monitoring infrastructure gaps mean pre-loading on the cavitation and fire flow dimensions is genuinely unknowable.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: There is a structural paradox in this system: the thesis's strongest surface evidence (high reservoir, healthy pressure, all pumps running) coexists with its greatest vulnerability (monitoring gaps that could mask kill switch breaches, single-unit degradation consuming redundancy margin, oversight framework dependent on the operator's self-reporting). The system looks most healthy precisely when the monitoring infrastructure is least capable of detecting problems. If the monitoring gaps were closed and all readings confirmed healthy, the thesis would be strongly confirmed. If the gaps were closed and revealed problems, the thesis could be near-falsified. The current state of confident-looking-but-partially-blind is the worst epistemic position — it provides false comfort without verification. The highest-risk scenario (P4 fails during peak demand with undetected P2/P3 issues) is simultaneously the scenario where the thesis would be most decisively tested.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals a system that is operationally functional but epistemically compromised. MWA's actions (documenting P4 degradation internally while publicly declaring optimality) are most likely standard institutional behavior rather than strategic deception, but the EFFECT is identical: external oversight operates on incomplete information. The most important finding is not any single operational metric — it is the structural configuration where 60% of kill switches cannot be fully evaluated, the primary operator controls the information flow, and the regulator lacks the telemetry to independently verify. Layer 4 should focus on resolving the baseline pressure question (which alone could swing the assessment from 'healthy with monitoring gaps' to 'near-falsified') and on determining whether the monitoring gaps are correctable (data exists but wasn't provided) or structural (sensors don't exist). The bear pressure of 35 is driven by unknown-unknowns behind data blind spots, not by adverse known readings — which makes the next data acquisition cycle disproportionately important.

knowledge_audit.signal: All four pumps operational within normal load range
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Thesis defines normal pump load as 70-85%. Data shows all four pumps within this range (74.8-78.5%). Kill switch #1 (Redundant Load Threshold) triggers at >115% aggregate for active units. Current aggregate ~304.6% across 4 pumps = 76.15% average. Well below any threshold. I have sufficient thesis knowledge to score this.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: No change. Direct verification against thesis thresholds confirms scoring.

knowledge_audit.signal: Pump 4 elevated vibration with seal weepage

However, 18.5 Hz is 53% above P1 baseline (12.1 Hz). Seal weepage is confirmed by maintenance log (Tier 1 originating source per LZ-EH-002). The combination of elevated vibration + seal failure + highest load on a single unit is a leading degradation indicator, not yet a kill switch breach but a trajectory concern. I understand the thesis well enough to score this.

_layer2_raw.bear_pressure_reasoning: Starting from previous score of 0. Current operational metrics are within normal ranges — all pumps running, pressure healthy, reservoir full, turbidity clean. This would suggest continued low bear pressure. HOWEVER, significant scoring weight comes from: (1) THREE of five kill switches have data gaps rendering them partially or fully unmonitorable (Kill Switches #2, #3, #5 — severity scores of 7, 7, and 8 respectively). Per LZ-EPH-002, confidence in thesis stability must be proportional to our ability to verify it, and we currently cannot verify 60% of our falsification criteria. (2) Pump 4 degradation (severity 5) combined with near-term maintenance (severity 7) and summer demand approach (severity 5) creates a compounding stress chain that could approach Kill Switch #1 within weeks. (3) MWA information asymmetry (severity 4) degrades external oversight effectiveness. Net assessment: the system APPEARS healthy on measurable indicators, but our monitoring aperture has critical blind spots. Bear pressure of 35 reflects moderate thesis stress driven primarily by monitoring infrastructure failure rather than operational failure — but monitoring failure can mask operational failure, which is why it scores meaningfully. This is NOT a surface-level reaction to P4 vibration headlines; it is a structural assessment of our ability to defend the thesis with available evidence.
_layer2_raw.layer2_summary: System operational metrics are currently within normal ranges — all four pumps running at 74-78% load, system pressure at 68.5 PSI with healthy margins, reservoir at 92.4%, water quality clean. The primary concern is NOT current operational failure but rather a convergence of three issues: (1) CRITICAL MONITORING GAPS — three of five kill switches (Cavitation, Fire Flow Collapse, Divergent Inventory Paradox) cannot be fully evaluated due to missing vibration data on P2/P3, missing sector hydrant data beyond Sector B, and missing baseline pressure data; (2) NEAR-TERM STRESS CHAIN — Pump 4 seal degradation → scheduled teardown → 3-pump operation at ~101.5% per unit → only 13.5 points of headroom to Kill Switch #1, all converging with seasonal demand increase; (3) INFORMATION ASYMMETRY — MWA's public framing ('operating optimally') contradicts internal maintenance reality, potentially leaving regulators and fire dispatch uninformed. Layer 3 should prioritize: acquiring baseline pressure data to resolve Kill Switch #5 status (this could change the entire assessment), obtaining P2/P3 vibration telemetry, verifying P4 teardown timeline, and assessing whether Fire Dispatch has independent monitoring capability.

action_reasoning: INCREASE_MONITORING is the only action consistent with CONTESTED thesis status. The system is not in crisis — no kill switch is breached and all measurable indicators are healthy — which rules out REDUCE_LOAD or EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. But the convergence of monitoring gaps, approaching P4 maintenance, and seasonal demand escalation means MAINTAIN_OPERATIONS provides insufficient visibility into developing risks. Priority monitoring actions: (1) obtain historical baseline pressure to resolve Kill Switch #5 status, (2) obtain P2/P3 vibration data, (3) confirm P4 teardown timeline, (4) obtain multi-sector hydrant pressure readings.

The Pump 4 degradation and upcoming maintenance window may fall entirely outside their monitoring aperture.

_layer1_raw.signal: Summer demand season approaching with ambient temp at 72°F
_layer1_raw.description: Ambient temperature at 72°F suggests spring/early-summer conditions. Summer peak demand will increase system draw significantly (lawn irrigation, cooling, increased consumption). The system currently runs at ~76% average pump load in what appears to be pre-peak conditions. Seasonal demand escalation could push loads toward upper normal range even before Pump 4 goes offline for maintenance.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Ambient temp 72°F; MWA statement references 'going into summer'; current pump loads already averaging 76%.

_layer1_raw.description: Turbidity at 0.8 NTU is well within EPA maximum of 4 NTU and ideal range (<1 NTU). This indicates reservoir source water quality and treatment processes are functioning normally. No contamination, sediment intrusion, or treatment plant issues are indicated.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Water turbidity at 0.8 NTU, below EPA limits and within ideal range.

_layer1_raw.signal: No baseline pressure data available for divergence tracking
_layer1_raw.description: The Divergent Inventory Paradox kill-switch requires detecting a >20% pressure drop from baseline, but no historical baseline pressure figure is provided in the current data feed. Without a reference point, this falsification criterion cannot be accurately monitored. If baseline were, say, 85 PSI, current 68.5 PSI would already represent a 19.4% decline — nearly triggering the paradox with reservoir at 92.4%.
_layer1_raw.evidence: No baseline_pressure field in data. Current pressure is 68.5 PSI. A baseline of ~85 PSI would put system at 19.4% decline, near the 20% threshold.

_layer1_raw.description: MWA is documented as 'historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs.' Whether MWA will execute this maintenance promptly or defer it (consistent with historical pattern) is unknown. Delay would mean running a degrading pump through peak summer demand.
_layer1_raw.evidence: MWA historical pattern per thesis context. Maintenance log uses 'scheduled' without specifying date. Seal weepage is an active condition.

_layer1_raw.signal: No alternative supply or competitive infrastructure signals
_layer1_raw.description: No evidence of alternative water sources, emergency interconnects with neighboring utilities, mobile treatment units, or private well expansion. The system appears to operate as a single-source monopoly from Reservoir Alpha through one pumping station. This concentration means any cascading pump failure has no external backup pathway, unlike systems with mutual aid agreements or redundant supply points.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Data shows single reservoir, single pump station, no references to interconnects or alternative supply arrangements.
